section.title: The Arrest and the Trial before Pilate
section.sectionRationale: The Passion narrative begins. In the garden Jesus gives Himself up with sovereign calm, the arresting party falling back at His I am He, and He shields His disciples. He is taken before Annas and Caiaphas while Peter denies Him three times by a charcoal fire. Then, before Pilate, the true nature of His kingship comes to light: a kingdom not of this world, a King who came to bear witness to the truth, while the crowd chooses Barabbas.
section.thesis: Jesus goes to His arrest in full command, laying down His life freely and drinking the cup the Father has given, protecting His own even as Peter denies Him, and before Pilate He reveals Himself as a King whose kingdom is not of this world and who came to bear witness to the truth, though the world prefers a violent man to the King of truth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the arrest in the garden and the I am He that fells the soldiers; Peter's first denial and Jesus before Annas; Peter's second and third denials; and the trial before Pilate, the kingdom not of this world, and the choice of Barabbas.

units.label: The arrest in the garden
units.note: I am He, and the cup the Father has given

verses.text: After Jesus had spoken these words, He went out with His disciples across the Kidron Valley, where they entered a garden.
Now Judas His betrayer also knew the place, because Jesus had often met there with His disciples.
So Judas brought a band of soldiers and officers from the chief priests and Pharisees. They arrived at the garden carrying lanterns, torches, and weapons.
Jesus, knowing all that was coming upon Him, stepped forward and asked them, “Whom are you seeking?”
“Jesus of Nazareth,” they answered. Jesus said, “{{egoeimi|I am He}}.” And Judas His betrayer was standing there with them.
When Jesus said, “I am He,” they drew back and fell to the ground.
So He asked them again, “Whom are you seeking?” “Jesus of Nazareth,” they answered.
“I told you that I am He,” Jesus replied. “So if you are looking for Me, let these men go.”
This was to fulfill the word He had spoken: “I have not lost one of those You have given Me.”
Then Simon Peter drew his sword and struck the servant of the high priest, cutting off his right ear. The servant’s name was Malchus.
“Put your sword back in its sheath!” Jesus said to Peter. “Shall I not drink the {{poterion|cup}} the Father has given Me?”
Then the band of soldiers, with its commander and the officers of the Jews, arrested Jesus and bound Him.
They brought Him first to Annas, who was the father-in-law of Caiaphas, the high priest that year.
Caiaphas was the one who had advised the Jews that it would be better if one man died for the people.
Now Simon Peter and another disciple were following Jesus. Since that disciple was known to the high priest, he also went with Jesus into the courtyard of the high priest.
But Peter stood outside at the door. Then the disciple who was known to the high priest went out and spoke to the doorkeeper, and brought Peter in.
At this, the servant girl watching the door said to Peter, “Aren’t you also one of this man’s disciples?” “I am not,” he answered.
Because it was cold, the servants and officers were standing around a charcoal fire they had made to keep warm. And Peter was also standing with them, warming himself.
Meanwhile, the high priest questioned Jesus about His disciples and His teaching.
“I have spoken openly to the world,” Jesus answered. “I always taught in the synagogues and at the temple, where all the Jews come together. I said nothing in secret.
Why are you asking Me? Ask those who heard My message. Surely they know what I said.”
When Jesus had said this, one of the officers standing nearby slapped Him in the face and said, “Is this how You answer the high priest?”
Jesus replied, “If I said something wrong, testify as to what was wrong. But if I spoke correctly, why did you strike Me?”
Then Annas sent Him, still bound, to Caiaphas the high priest.
Simon Peter was still standing and warming himself. So they asked him, “Aren’t you also one of His disciples?” He denied it and said, “I am not.”
One of the high priest’s servants, a relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off, asked, “Didn’t I see you with Him in the garden?”
Peter denied it once more, and immediately a rooster crowed.

units.label: Jesus before Pilate
units.note: My kingdom is not of this world

verses.text: Then they led Jesus away from Caiaphas into the Praetorium. By now it was early morning, and the Jews did not enter the Praetorium, to avoid being defiled and unable to eat the Passover.
So Pilate went out to them and asked, “What accusation are you bringing against this man?”
“If He were not a criminal,” they replied, “we would not have handed Him over to you.”
“You take Him and judge Him by your own law,” Pilate told them. “We are not permitted to execute anyone,” the Jews replied.
This was to fulfill the word that Jesus had spoken to indicate the kind of death He was going to die.
Pilate went back into the Praetorium, summoned Jesus, and asked Him, “Are You the King of the Jews?”
“Are you saying this on your own,” Jesus asked, “or did others tell you about Me?”
“Am I a Jew?” Pilate replied. “Your own people and chief priests handed You over to me. What have You done?”
Jesus answered, “My {{basileia|kingdom}} is not of this world; if it were, My servants would fight to prevent My arrest by the Jews. But now My kingdom is not of this realm.”

“Then You are a king!” Pilate said. “You say that I am a king,” Jesus answered. “For this reason I was born and have come into the world, to testify to the {{aletheia|truth}}. Everyone who belongs to the truth listens to My voice.”
“What is truth?” Pilate asked. And having said this, he went out again to the Jews and told them, “I find no basis for a charge against Him.
But it is your custom that I release to you one prisoner at the Passover. So then, do you want me to release to you the King of the Jews?”
“Not this man,” they shouted, “but Barabbas!” (Now Barabbas was an insurrectionist.)

egoeimi.gloss: I am, or I am He. When Jesus answers the arresting party with I am, they draw back and fall to the ground, a flash of the divine majesty behind the plain words, echoing the great I am. Even at His arrest, He is not seized against His will; He gives Himself up, and His word alone momentarily overpowers the armed band.

poterion.gloss: Cup. Jesus speaks of the cup the Father has given Him to drink, the Old Testament image of the cup of God's wrath against sin. To drink it is to bear that judgment in the place of His people. His willingness, shall I not drink the cup, shows His death is a chosen obedience, not a tragic accident.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, kingship, reign. Jesus tells Pilate His kingdom is not of this world, not sourced in or advanced by worldly power and violence, which is why His servants do not fight. It is no less real for that; He is truly a King, but His reign comes through truth and self-giving, not conquest, and it claims the allegiance of all who belong to the truth.

aletheia.gloss: Truth. Jesus says He was born and came into the world to bear witness to the truth, and that everyone who belongs to the truth hears His voice. Pilate's weary reply, what is truth, stands as the world's evasion before the One who is Himself the truth. Christ's kingship is exercised precisely by testifying to the truth.

basic.context: After the prayer, Jesus crosses into a garden, where Judas leads an armed party to arrest Him. Jesus steps forward and asks whom they seek; when He says I am He, they fall back to the ground. He gives Himself up but insists His disciples go free, and He stops Peter's sword, saying He must drink the cup the Father has given. Taken to Annas and Caiaphas, He is questioned and struck, while outside Peter denies knowing Him three times by a charcoal fire. Before Pilate, Jesus explains that His kingdom is not of this world and that He came to bear witness to the truth. Pilate finds no guilt, but the crowd demands Barabbas.
basic.hermeneutics: John shows Jesus in complete command even as He is arrested and tried; He is the one laying down His life, not a helpless victim. The key questions are, what kind of King is Jesus, and what is His kingdom. His kingdom is real but not of this world, not spread by force, and He reigns by bearing witness to the truth. Peter's denials, set against Jesus' bold confession, warn of the weakness of self-confidence. Pilate's what is truth captures the world's evasion before Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a few words. Jesus' I am, ego eimi, momentarily fells the arresting party. He speaks of the poterion, the cup, of God's wrath He must drink. Before Pilate He declares His basileia, kingdom, is not of this world, and that He came to bear witness to the aletheia, the truth.
basic.typology: The garden of the arrest recalls the garden of Eden, where the first Adam fell; here the last Adam stands firm. The cup of wrath fulfills the prophetic image of the cup God's enemies must drink, now drunk by the Son in His people's place. Barabbas released while Jesus is condemned pictures the guilty going free because the innocent takes his place.
basic.christ: Christ is the sovereign, self-giving Lord who lays down His life, drinks the cup of wrath, protects His own, and reigns as the King of truth whose kingdom is not of this world. He stands innocent where Barabbas the guilty goes free.
basic.god: God the Father gives the cup, and the Son willingly drinks it, so that the whole Passion unfolds within the Father's purpose. God's kingdom advances not by the sword but by the truth and the cross.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 51 speaks of the cup of God's wrath. Luke 22 records the agony over the cup. John 6 and 17 promised Jesus would lose none. Revelation 19 shows Christ the King who conquers by truth.
basic.oneStory: In a garden the last Adam stands where the first fell, and He gives Himself to drink the cup of wrath His people deserved. Before earthly power He reveals a kingdom not of this world, ruled by truth, and the guilty Barabbas goes free while the innocent King is condemned, a picture of the whole gospel of substitution.

context.literary: The opening of John's Passion narrative, marked by Jesus' sovereign initiative at every turn: He steps forward, He fells the guards, He protects His disciples, He rebukes Peter, He answers His judges. John interweaves Peter's denials with Jesus' trials, contrasting the disciple's failure with the Master's faithful witness. The Pilate scene is structured as a series of movements in and out of the Praetorium, probing the nature of Jesus' kingship.
context.historical: The garden lay across the Kidron on the Mount of Olives. Annas, a former high priest and power behind the office, was questioned first, then Caiaphas. The Jewish authorities, under Roman rule, could not carry out executions, so they brought Jesus to Pilate, the Roman governor. Their refusal to enter the Praetorium to avoid ritual defilement before Passover is a pointed irony. Barabbas was an insurrectionist, a violent man, whose release throws Jesus' peaceful kingship into relief.
context.canonical: The cup fulfills the Old Testament image of the cup of God's wrath, Isaiah 51:17 and Jeremiah 25:15, drunk by Christ for His people. The kingdom not of this world reframes messianic kingship away from political revolt, consistent with Daniel 7's everlasting kingdom given through the Son of Man. Jesus as witness to the truth connects to John's prologue and to Revelation 1:5, the faithful witness. The release of Barabbas dramatizes the substitution at the heart of the atonement, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus going to His death in sovereign freedom and obedience, drinking the cup the Father gives, protecting His own, and bearing witness before Pilate to a kingdom not of this world and to the truth, so that readers see the true nature of His kingship and His willing sacrifice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the events of the arrest and trial. Its abiding truths are that Jesus laid down His life willingly, that His kingdom is not advanced by worldly force, and that He is the King of truth whose voice His people heed; Peter's denial stands as a warning against self-confidence for every disciple.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean that His kingdom is not of this world?

views.label: Not sourced in or spread by worldly power
views.case: Jesus explains that if His kingdom were of this world, His servants would fight; its origin and method are not the sword and political conquest, though it is a real and present reign.

views.label: Not a denial of any earthly relevance
views.case: The kingdom is not of this world in source and character, yet it lays claim on this world and will one day be manifest in it; it is otherworldly in origin but not escapist or irrelevant.

debates.bearing: Both guard against two errors: treating Christ's kingdom as a political program advanced by force, and treating it as having no bearing on this world; it is a real reign, from above, exercised through truth and destined to fill the earth.

debates.question: How significant is the release of Barabbas?

views.label: A vivid picture of substitution
views.case: The guilty insurrectionist goes free while the innocent Jesus is condemned in his place, a living parable of the gospel, that the sinner is released because the sinless one takes his condemnation.

views.label: An exposure of the world's preference
views.case: The crowd's choice of a violent man over the King of truth exposes the world's blindness and rebellion, choosing the way of the sword over the way of God.

debates.bearing: Both readings enrich the scene: Barabbas's release both pictures the substitutionary heart of the cross and exposes a world that prefers violence to the King who came to bear witness to the truth.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is threaded with revealing words. When Jesus answers the arresting party with ego eimi, I am, the plain reply carries a flash of divine majesty, and the armed band draws back and falls to the ground; He is not seized against His will but gives Himself up. He speaks of the poterion, the cup, the Old Testament image of God's wrath against sin, which He must drink in His people's place, so that His death is willing obedience, not accident. Before Pilate He declares that His basileia, His kingdom, is not of this world, not sourced in or spread by force, and that He was born to bear witness to the aletheia, the truth, so that everyone who belongs to the truth hears His voice, while Pilate's what is truth voices the world's evasion.

typology.type: The garden where the first Adam fell
typology.fulfillment: The garden where the last Adam stands firm and gives Himself up in obedience, John 18:1-11 and Romans 5:19.

typology.type: The cup of God's wrath, Isaiah 51:17
typology.fulfillment: The cup the Father gives the Son to drink, bearing judgment for His people, John 18:11.
The guilty set free because the sinless Christ takes their place, John 18:40 and 2 Corinthians 5:21.

study.christ: Christ is revealed as sovereign even in His arrest. He steps forward, speaks, and the armed band falls to the ground at His I am; He is the one laying down His life, not a victim overpowered. He shields His disciples, insisting they go free, and He stops Peter's sword because He has resolved to drink the cup the Father has given. Before the high priest and Pilate He answers with dignity and truth, revealing Himself as the King whose kingdom is not of this world and who came to bear witness to the truth. He stands innocent, declared blameless even by Pilate, in the place of the guilty Barabbas who goes free.
study.god: The Father is the one who gives the cup, so that the whole ordeal of the arrest and trial unfolds within His sovereign purpose and the Son's willing obedience. God's kingdom is shown to advance not by the sword but by truth and self-giving love, a reign from above that does not depend on worldly power. The scene reveals a God whose ways confound the world: He conquers not by fighting but by suffering, and He saves the guilty by letting the innocent stand condemned in their place, so that even the release of a violent man serves to display the substitution at the heart of redemption.

crossReferences.note: The cup of God's wrath, which Jesus speaks of drinking as the cup the Father has given Him.
Father, if You are willing, take this cup from Me, the agony over the same cup Jesus resolves to drink.
God made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, the substitution pictured in Barabbas's release.
Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, who bore witness to the truth before Pilate.

study.oneStory: In a garden, where the first Adam fell to temptation, the last Adam stands firm and freely gives Himself up to drink the cup of wrath His people deserved. Arrested and tried, He remains sovereign throughout, protecting His own and answering His judges with the truth. Before Rome's power He unveils a kingdom not of this world, a reign exercised not by the sword but by bearing witness to the truth. And when the crowd chooses the violent Barabbas over the King of truth, the innocent is condemned so the guilty may go free, a living picture of the substitution by which the whole story of salvation moves toward the cross.

quiz.q: What happens when Jesus answers the arresting party with I am He?

options.text: They draw back and fall to the ground, a flash of His divine majesty.
options.note: Verse 6 shows the momentary display of majesty behind the words.

options.text: They fall back briefly and then flee in terror.
options.note: They do draw back and fall down, but they do not flee; they proceed with the arrest Jesus permits.

options.text: They laugh and mock Him.
options.note: They fall to the ground, awed, not mocking at this point.

options.text: They are momentarily overpowered by His word.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific detail that they drew back and fell to the ground.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus tell Peter to put away his sword?

options.text: Because He has resolved to drink the cup the Father has given Him.
options.note: Verse 11 grounds His restraint in His willing obedience to the Father's cup.

options.text: Because He is afraid of the soldiers.
options.note: He acts in sovereign resolve, not fear.

options.text: Because violence would embarrass His movement.
options.note: The real reason is His purpose to drink the cup, not concern about appearances.

options.text: Because He does not want His disciples harmed.
options.note: He does protect His disciples, but the stated reason for stopping the sword is His resolve to drink the cup.

quiz.q: How does Jesus describe His kingdom to Pilate?

options.text: It is not of this world; if it were, His servants would fight.
options.note: Verse 36 defines His kingdom as not sourced in or spread by worldly force.

options.text: It is a political kingdom to overthrow Rome.
options.note: Many expected exactly this, but Jesus denies it; His kingdom is not of this world and His servants do not fight.

options.text: It does not really exist at all.
options.note: He affirms He is a King with a real kingdom, just not of this world.

options.text: It is a reign that does not come by force.
options.note: True, but it omits the key phrase, that His kingdom is not of this world.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say He came into the world to do?

options.text: To testify to the truth, so that everyone who belongs to the truth hears His voice.
options.note: Verse 37 defines His mission as bearing witness to the truth.

options.text: To raise an army and seize the throne.
options.note: His kingdom is not of this world; He came to bear witness to the truth.

options.text: To establish an earthly reign of truth and justice.
options.note: His kingdom is bound up with the truth, but He came to bear witness to the truth, not to set up an earthly reign.

options.text: To reveal the truth to the world.
options.note: True, but it omits that those who belong to the truth hear His voice, the mark of His subjects.

quiz.q: Whom does the crowd choose when Pilate offers to release Jesus?

options.text: Barabbas, an insurrectionist, rather than Jesus.
options.note: Verse 40 records the choice of the violent Barabbas over Jesus.

options.text: They ask for Jesus to be released.
options.note: They demand Barabbas, not Jesus.

options.text: They reluctantly let Pilate make the choice.
options.note: They do not leave it to Pilate; they actively shout for Barabbas over Jesus.

options.text: A violent man instead of the King of truth.
options.note: True and pointed, but it does not name Barabbas, the specific choice the text records.

questions: Even at His arrest Jesus was in complete command, laying down His life freely. How does it change your view of the cross to see Jesus as the willing giver, not the helpless victim?
Jesus resolved to drink the cup the Father gave Him. What cup of hardship is God asking you to accept from His hand, trusting His purpose in it?
Peter denied Jesus by a fire while Jesus boldly confessed the truth. Where are you tempted to quietly distance yourself from Christ to stay comfortable?
Jesus' kingdom is not advanced by force but by truth. How does that reshape the way you think you should defend and spread His cause?
The crowd chose Barabbas over the King of truth. Where do you see the world, or your own heart, still preferring lesser things to Christ?

translationNotes.note: I have not lost one of those You have given Me recalls Jesus' words in John 6:39 and 17:12; His protection of the disciples at His arrest fulfills His own promise.
The cup the Father has given Me draws on the Old Testament image of the cup of God's wrath, which Jesus willingly drinks for His people.
The kind of death He was going to die refers to crucifixion; because the Jews could not execute, Jesus would die by the Roman method He had foretold in John 12:32-33.
Barabbas was an insurrectionist; his release in place of the innocent Jesus vividly pictures the substitution at the heart of the gospel.
